title: An Efficient Local Search Approach for Polarized Community Discovery in Signed Networks
abstract: Signed networks, where edges are labeled as positive or negative to represent friendly or antagonistic interactions, offer a natural framework for analyzing polarization, trust, and conflict in social systems. Detecting meaningful group structures in such networks is crucial for understanding online discourse, political divisions, and trust dynamics. A key challenge is to identify communities that are internally cohesive and externally antagonistic, while allowing for neutral or unaligned vertices. In this paper, we propose a method for identifying $k$ polarized communities that addresses a major limitation of prior methods: their tendency to produce highly size-imbalanced solutions. We introduce a novel optimization objective that avoids such imbalance. In addition, it is well known that approximation algorithms based on *local search* are highly effective for clustering signed networks when neutral vertices are not allowed. We build on this idea and design the first local search algorithm that extends to the setting with neutral vertices while scaling to large networks. By connecting our approach to block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe optimization, we prove a linear convergence rate, enabled by the structure of our objective. Experiments on real-world and synthetic datasets demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in solution quality, while remaining competitive in computational efficiency.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper presents an efficient local search method for detecting polarized communities in signed networks. The authors propose a novel formulation of the Polarized Community Discovery (PCD) problem, addressing issues of community balance. The approach leverages block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe optimization and translates it into a local search procedure with provable convergence guarantees. Through extensive experiments on real-world datasets, the proposed method demonstrates superior solution quality and computational efficiency compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

S1) The paper is overall well-written

S2) The proposed approach based on Frank-Wolfe optimization appears to be novel, to the best of my knowledge. To support the claim that “in Section 4, we demonstrate across a range of real-world and synthetic datasets that the advantages of local search optimization (e.g., for CC) carry over to PCD as well,” the authors could strengthen the connection to prior work by referencing related studies on correlation clustering (CC) where local search procedures have been successfully employed [n1, n2].

[n1] An Efficient Local Search Algorithm for Correlation Clustering on Large Graphs. COCOA 2023.

[n2] In and out: Optimizing overall interaction in probabilistic graphs under clustering constraints. KDD 2020.

S3) The paper offers several valuable contributions across multiple aspects: theoretical (including a novel problem formulation and theoretical results), algorithmic (proposing algorithms to solve the introduced problem), and experimental (presenting extensive results in both the main paper and the appendix).

S4) Convergence analysis of the proposed local search algorithm.

S5) Experiments on both real-world and synthetic datasets. The selected datasets are consistent with those found in the relevant literature on PCD in signed graphs.

S6) The results confirm the significance of the proposed method, as it yields solutions with high polarity while achieving better size balance.

Weaknesses:

W1) The paper fails to compare with N2PC (equipped with \gamma-polarity) on synthetic networks with k = 2 and unbalanced ground-truth communities. Since the proposed method and N2PC are the only methods that account for community balance, such comparison seems needed. Also, I think Table 1 should report for k = 2 the results of N2PC.

Overall, I believe this is a solid piece of work in which the strengths outweigh the weaknesses, and for this reason, I recommend acceptance.

QUESTIONS:
Q1) Could the authors clarify why a comparison with N2PC (equipped with \gamma-polarity) on synthetic networks with k = 2 and unbalanced ground-truth communities was omitted, given that both the proposed method and N2PC are specifically designed to account for community balance?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The submission studies the question of identifying polarized communities in signed graphs. In this setting, each community consists of two opposing groups. The main point of the submission is that in previous works, the identified communities can have opposing groups of highly imbalanced sizes. Thus, the submission proposes a novel objective function, which obtains more balanced communities.

For the novel objective, an optimization algorithm based on the block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe method is proposed. It is then shown that this is equivalent to a local search approach, which is proven to converge to a stationary point and results on the convergence speed are provided. Further, a more efficient variant of this algorithm is provided.

The proposed algorithm is evaluated on synthetic and on real-world data, and it is compared against state-of-the-art competitors.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
1. Identifying polarized communities in signed graphs is an interesting problem, especially as many existing methods can be quite fragile w.r.t. the solution quality they deliver. Thus, providing new methods in this area is interesting.
2. The submission is overall well-written and easy to follow.
3. Theoretical guarantees are presented, and the new method has strong mathematical foundations. In general, the submission provides good insights into the connections between continuous and discrete optimization.

Weaknesses:
1. Regarding the motivation of the submission, it is not fully clear to me why one should be aiming to find communities which are all roughly of the same size. I do agree that obtaining just very large or very small clusters is not ideal, but why should they all have the same size?
2.  Continuing from the point above: In the experiments when defining the imbalance factor, why is the parameter $\xi$ set to 3? It appears to be somewhat arbitrary, and I wonder whether this has been done in a principled way?
3. It is not surprising that local search methods converge to stationary points. Also, the way that the new objective function is derived (by penalizing too many points in the non-neutral clusters) is quite standard.

QUESTIONS:
1. Regarding the motivation of the submission, it is not fully clear to me why one should be aiming to find communities which are all roughly of the same size. I do agree that obtaining just very large or very small clusters is not ideal, but why should they all have the same size?
2.  Continuing from the point above: In the experiments when defining the imbalance factor, why is the parameter $\xi$ set to 3? It appears to be somewhat arbitrary, and I wonder whether this has been done in a principled way?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper addresses the Polarized Community Discovery Problem in Signed Networks, which consists of partitioning the network into k clusters with high (positive) similarity within clusters, low (negative) similarity across clusters and a possibly neutral subset of nodes. The paper proposes an alternative objective (with parameters alpha and beta) to a previously applied objective (polarity) and shows that the relaxation of the objective (originally NP-bard) can be formulated as a Block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe Optimization. The FW formulation is then shown to be equivalent to a local search algorithm that can be implemented e"ciently. A theoretical analysis of the impact of the parameters alpha and beta is also provided. The experiments compare the proposed solution against four alternatives using eight datasets, showing that the baselines achieve worse results in terms of the proposed objective while the proposed solution is competitive with respect to polarity.

I have to disclose that I have reviewed a previous submission and rebuttal of this paper earlier this year. Besides clarifications, the reviews and rebuttal focused on limitations in the evaluation of the proposed approach that have been partially addressed in this new version.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
**Strengths:**

- The relationship between the FW formulation and the local search algorithm is interesting.
- The paper presents many additional experiments and analysis in the appendix.
- The proposed optimizations significantly decrease the complexity of the local search heuristic.

**Weaknesses:**
- The experimental section does not provide strong support for the proposed formulation.
- The paper addresses a problem with limited interest within the ML community.
- The proposed algorithm does not provide any approximation guarantees.

**Detailed comments:**

I find this to be an interesting paper, with some innovative ideas and a few limitations regarding its practical implications. I will provide some details on the weaknesses of the paper to help the authors to develop their rebuttal and possibly improve the paper.

*Empirical support for the proposed formulation:* The formulation proposed in the paper (Equation 3) is motivated using theoretical arguments. Instead, it would be more interesting to use real-world ground-truth data to justify the proposed formulation. More specifically, how does the proposed formulation can potentially impact real-world applications in social or communication networks? Otherwise, it is hard to argue for one formulation versus another. In formulations for sparsest and normalized cuts, the balance appears as a ratio and not an additive form. Spectral graph theory supports interesting connections between these formulations and Laplacian eigenvectors. It is not clear when and why we should move to an additive balancing factor as proposed in this paper.

*Problem:* Interest in polarization in social networks peaked about an eight years ago, together with misinformation and other topics. A lot of work has been done since but it is not clear where this line of research is moving towards. Looking at the NeurIPS topics of interest, this paper is closest to unsupervised machine learning and perhaps optimization. Other venues such as WetConf, WSDM, etc. seem to be more focused on this line of research.

*Theoretical guarantees:* While the problem studied in this paper is np-hard, it is not clear if it can be approximated with any theoretical guarantee and whether the proposed algorithm achieves such a guarantee.

QUESTIONS:
Q1. How can the proposed formulation be justified based on real-world applications?

Q2. Why is this paper a good match for NeurIPS?

Q3. Is the optimization problem considered in this paper hard to approximate? If not, does the proposed algorithm provide any approximation guarantee?

LIMITATIONS:
Partially. The main paper could include a paragraph discussing these aspects.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper proposes a polarized community discovery method for signed graphs, aiming to address imbalanced clustering issues while maintaining good polarity of the discovered cohesive communities. Experiments on real and synthetic graphs were conducted to verify the performance of the method.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
S1: The problem studied in the paper is well-motivated and highly practical for real applications.

S2: The paper is clearly written and easy to follow.

S3: Detailed proofs and analyses are provided in the appendices.

W1: It would be better to include performance comparisons with the most recent methods. Among all compared algorithms, only one was recently proposed (in 2024); all others were proposed before 2020.

W2: The experiments could be enhanced by including larger datasets.

W3: The related works are not comprehensively surveyed.

QUESTIONS:
Q1: The paper claims that scalability is one of the main contributions of the proposed method. However, the largest dataset used in the paper has only 2 million edges, which is not convincing to me. It would be better to include larger real datasets in the main paper to showcase the efficiency of the proposed method.

Q2: It would be beneficial to demonstrate performance by comparing with related works using more sophisticated polarized community models, e.g., k-core, k-truss, and cliques. These methods can easily be extended to support top-k community retrieval. For example:

[1] Effective and efficient core computation in signed networks, Information Sciences 2023

[2] Maximum Balanced (k,ϵ)-Bitruss Detection in Signed Bipartite Graph, VLDB 2024

[3] Clique Identification in Signed Graphs: A Balance Theory Based Model, TKDE 2023

[4] Identifying Large Structural Balanced Cliques in Signed Graphs, TKDE 2024

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
This paper addresses the problem of discovering polarized communities in signed graphs. The authors propose a novel objective function with additive regularization and solve it using a block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe optimization approach. The paper is clearly written, with strong theoretical contributions and extensive experimental evaluation. Although the reviewers concerned insufficient comparisons with recent methods and limited dataset sizes in the experiments, the proposed formulation and its connection to an efficient local search algorithm via Frank-Wolfe optimization are both interesting and valuable. Therefore, I recommend acceptance of this paper.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
